coredata.dc:title: Microwave synthesized N-doped carbon dots for dual mode detection of Hg(II) ion and degradation of malachite green dye
coredata.dc:creator: Thara, Chinnu R
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: One of the most intriguing materials today is carbon dots, which offer a variety of possible uses owing to their distinct photophysical and chemical characteristics. The current study examines the electrochemical and photochemical aspects of carbon dots produced in a single pot for environmental sustainability. Domestic microwave-assisted pyrolysis of urea and glucose yielded chemically synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (microwave synthesized N-doped carbon dots (M-NCDs)) with blue fluorescence and a quantum yield of 14.9 %. High water dispersibility, stability, and biocompatibility were the significant attributes of synthesized M-NCDs. Customarily fluorescent carbon dots were initially used for sensing studies. Fluorescent and electrochemical studies manifest the excellent stability, sensitivity, and selectivity of M-NCDs for mercuric ions. Both methods' Hg (II) procure detection limits of 3.5 nM and 6.1 nM. In addition to sensing traits, the subsequent section deals with the potential of M-NDCs to bring about the exhaustive degradation of malachite green (MG) dye. Within 60 min, 98 % of the dye was catalytically degraded by M-NCD by first-order kinetics based on the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model. This is the first time reporting the catalytic degradation of malachite green dye utilizing carbon dot in its natural form rather than being doped with any metal atom or converted to any composite form.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave synthesis
Carbon dots
Mercuric ion
Fluorescent sensing
Electrochemical sensing
Malachite degradation
Catalytic degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microwave assisted synthesis of N-doped carbon dots (M-NCDs)
Catalytic degradation of malachite green with M-NCD

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Chinnu Thara is grateful to Council of Scientific & Industrial Research (CSIR) for Senior Research Fellowship (Fellowship No: 09/499(0105)/2018-EMR-I ) and Department of Science & Technology (DST), Government of India for instrumentation facilities provided by DST-PURSE Phase II to Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala, India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological, optical and structural properties: (a) HR-TEM image showing quasi-spherical morphology, (b) combination of UV–vis. absorption, excitation-emission spectra, and photograph of M-NCD in short UV, (c) XRD pattern, (d) High resolution XPS spectra C1s, (e) O1s, and (f) N1s spectra.

Sensing of Hg(II) with M-NCD: (a) fluorescent sensing with different metal ions and quenching of fluorescence with Hg(II), (b) fluorescence quenching of M-NCD at different concentrations of Hg(II) (c) plot of Hg(II) concentration Vs. I0/I of M-NCD, (d) electrochemical sensing of Hg(II) using CV, (e) DPV of M-NCD at different concentrations of Hg(II), and (f) plot of Hg(II)concentration vs. current from DPV.
Catalytic degradation of MG: (a) UV–vis. absorption spectra showing degradation with time, (b) plot of ln (C/C0) vs. time, and (c) degradation efficiency of control, dark, and light reaction.
(a) HPLC analysis at different stages of reaction, and (b) possible mechanism of degradation.
Schematic representation of the mechanism of catalytic degradation of MG.

head.ce:title: Microwave synthesized N-doped carbon dots for dual mode detection of Hg(II) ion and degradation of malachite green dye

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, 686560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, 686560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One of the most intriguing materials today is carbon dots, which offer a variety of possible uses owing to their distinct photophysical and chemical characteristics. The current study examines the electrochemical and photochemical aspects of carbon dots produced in a single pot for environmental sustainability. Domestic microwave-assisted pyrolysis of urea and glucose yielded chemically synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (microwave synthesized N-doped carbon dots (M-NCDs)) with blue fluorescence and a quantum yield of 14.9 %. High water dispersibility, stability, and biocompatibility were the significant attributes of synthesized M-NCDs. Customarily fluorescent carbon dots were initially used for sensing studies. Fluorescent and electrochemical studies manifest the excellent stability, sensitivity, and selectivity of M-NCDs for mercuric ions. Both methods' Hg (II) procure detection limits of 3.5 nM and 6.1 nM. In addition to sensing traits, the subsequent section deals with the potential of M-NDCs to bring about the exhaustive degradation of malachite green (MG) dye. Within 60 min, 98 % of the dye was catalytically degraded by M-NCD by first-order kinetics based on the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model. This is the first time reporting the catalytic degradation of malachite green dye utilizing carbon dot in its natural form rather than being doped with any metal atom or converted to any composite form.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The environment is agonized due to the immense advancements made by humanity in industry, domestic life, and technology. Aquatic pollution is particularly detrimental because it immediately affects all life forms. Every year, tonnes of pollutants from home and the workplace enter the marine environment [1]. A significant source of environmental contamination is heavy metals. Human activity, which includes mining and smelting operations, industrial production and consumption, as well as domestic and agricultural usage of metals and metal-containing compounds, is one of the primary sources of heavy metal pollution [2,3]. Heavy metal Mercury is a persistent environmental toxin and contaminant that harms various biological functions and tissues. It is a typical component of electrical equipment like batteries, switches, and thermostats.

Additionally, it is utilized in numerous industrial operations, such as producing caustic soda, nuclear reactors, wood processing, solvent for reactive and precious metals, and pharmaceutical product preservation. Because mercury is so pervasive in the environment, it poses a risk to people, plants, and animals. The heart, kidneys, brain, and other metabolic processes are all seriously endangered by mercury. Based on the material's chemical activity and biological properties, the molecular mechanism of toxicity suggests that oxidative stress is a factor in the material's toxicity [4,5].

In addition to heavy metal contamination, dyes are more frequent pollutants from industries and other resources. Dyes significantly affect photosynthetic activity in aquatic life by reducing light penetration. Despite low quantities, they are also extraordinarily poisonous and can cause cancer and mutagenesis in living things. Dyes usually have a synthetic origin and complex aromatic molecular structures, which make them chemically stable with apparently no biodegradability. Malachite Green (MG) is a basic water-soluble dye belonging to the triphenylmethane family. It is extensively employed in food, health, textile, and other sectors for various reasons, including as a parasiticide in aquaculture. This dye exhibits high cytotoxicity in mammalian cells and can cause cell transformation and lipid peroxidation [6]. Various applications of MG dye in the environment always leave enough traces to affect humans and other living organisms. From an environmental standpoint, there is consistent concern about the fate of malachite green in aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems. Thus, it becomes necessary to identify the toxic MG in the environment and a suitable method for decomposing these components. MG are not readily degradable and are typically not removed from water by treatment and conventional methods like adsorption, ultrafiltration, and chemical and electrochemical [7,8]. Degradation is an effective and rapid technique to remove pollutants from wastewater. In this process, dyes are broken down, ideally into innocuous products. Catalytic degradation of dyes is particularly advantageous over the conventional methods because they are eco-friendly, low cost, involve quick oxidation, no formation of polycyclic products, and complete oxidation of pollutants [9]. Recently, many reports have been on the degradation application with carbon dots, especially photocatalytic degradation of several contaminants. Due to their superior light-trapping capabilities, excellent efficiency in separating photo-generated charge carriers and significant absorption of visible light, carbon dots can be considered effective photocatalysts in degrading organic dyes [10]. Also, it's well-known that carbon dots are advantageous for using environmentally benign fluorescence probes for sensing even trace amounts of analytes [11,12].

The current finding outlines a more straightforward method for producing N-doped carbon dots using a domestic microwave oven. The M-NCDs served as a dual-purpose nano-platform for the fluorescent and electrochemical detection of Hg (II). And concurrently, it performed as a catalyst for the depletion of malachite green dye in water bodies. The sensing part of the study focuses on pH dependence, detection limit, stability, selectivity, and sensitivity of M-NCDs for the respective methods. The other half of the study dealt with an extensive understanding of MG degradation with synthesized carbon dots. This carbon dot is significantly distinct from other carbon dots that act as a photocatalyst in degradation reactions. Our catalyst's key benefit is that it is a plain carbon dot with no metal or polymer decorations, which results in thorough MG degradation. In addition to being fundamentally simple, it can degrade a complicated dye structure in 60 min without outside variables like light, heat, and co-catalysts.

Urea, d-glucose, Quinine sulfate, sodium phosphate monobasic, sodium phosphate dibasic, sodium hydroxide, and hydrochloric acid, metal chlorides namely potassium chloride (KCl), mercuric chloride (HgCl2), copper chloride (CuCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), ferric chloride (FeCl2), lead chloride (PbCl2), cadmium chloride (CdCl2) and chromium chloride (CrCl3), silver chloride (AgCl), zinc chloride (ZnCl2) potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), and malachite green dye of analytical grade, were purchased from Merck (India).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave assisted synthesis of N-doped carbon dots (M-NCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthesis, 2g of urea and 1g of d-glucose were uniformly mixed in 20 mL of double distilled water. The clear solution obtained was then subjected to microwave irradiation for 15 min in a domestic microwave oven (Sharp R-219 T (W)), 2450 MHz). During the treatment in oven initially all the water gets evaporated leaving off-white colored precipitate which then gets charred give the final product M-NCD. This product was dispersed in distilled water (50 mL) and sonicated. In the purification stage, initially the solution was centrifuged followed by filtration through 0.43 μm membrane to remove bigger particles [13]. This filtered solution was further purified by dialysis in ultra-pure water for 48 h. Synthesis scheme is given in SI (Fig.Scheme 1).

Based on a previously established methodology that compares integrated PL intensities, quantum yield was determined. The reference was chosen to be 0.1 M H2SO4 of quinine sulfate, with a quantum yield of 54 %. With absorbance below 0.10 at 360 nm, M-NCD and reference solutions were made at five different concentrations. In order to calculate the quantum yield, the following equation was used:
[1]
Φ S
=
Φ R
×
μ s
μ R
×
(
η s
η R
)
2
… … … … …

Where the quantum yield of the sample, and the reference represented as "ΦS", and"ΦR". "μs and μR" represent the slope of the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance plot of sample and reference, respectively, and refractive indices of solutions of sample and reference are referred as "ηR and ηs".

A typical test used double-distilled water to dilute 100 μL of M-NCD to 3 mL of suspension before adding various Hg (II) doses. The corresponding fluorescence quenching spectra with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm at room temperature were eventually acquired after the mixed solutions were maintained static for a few minutes. The fluorescence intensity of plane M-NCD is designated as I0, and the fluorescence intensity after adding metal ions is denoted as I.

All Biologic SP 200 workstation investigations were conducted using a three-electrode setup. Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) served as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl served as the reference electrode, and platinum wire served as the counter electrode. The bare GCE was polished with alumina slurry of 0.05 M. For the analysis, about 50 μg/mL of the N- doped carbon dot solution was dispersed in Nafion solution (0.5 %). Aliquot (10 μL) from the mother solution was drop casted on the GCE surface and allowed to dry naturally. The analysis was done in an inert atmosphere at room temperature.
Fluorescence and electrochemical sensing techniques were tested for M-NCDs ability to detect Hg (II) in real water samples.

For the photodegradation experiment, about 1000 μg/mL of the catalyst was taken in a 100 mL beaker, and 50 mL of MG (0.0001 M) solution was added. The mixture was sonicated for 2 min to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. About 2 mL of the catalyst containing dye solution was taken immediately after sonication and labelled dark. The bulk catalyst-dye mixture was kept static, and 2 mL of the bulk solution was withdrawn at regular intervals. A spectrophotometer was used to measure the change in absorbance of MG solution after catalyst treatment. The degradation efficiency of the photocatalyst was measured by using the following equation
[2]
Degradation
efficiency
( % )
=
(
C 0
−
C t
)
/
C 0
× 100
… … … … .
where C0 is the starting MG concentration and Ct is the MG concentration after t minutes.

HR-TEM images of N-doped carbon dots display a quasi-spherical structure with significantly less dispersion (Fig. 1 a). Average particle size of M-NCD obtained after considering almost a hundred particles was a 3.09 nm determined from particle size histogram (Fig. S1a). The lattice spacing of 0.27 nm corresponding to the C(100) plane of graphitic carbon could be seen in the high-resolution images (Fig. S1b) [14]. SAED pattern with diffused disk patterns pinpointed the amorphous nature of M-NCD (Fig. S1c). The carbon dots had two significant absorptions in the UV–vis. spectrum, one at 217 nm and a broad one around 272 nm; these absorptions correspond to the π–π* transition of C=C (sp2) bonds, and n–π* transition of C=O, and N–H groups. A broad excitation peak in the excitation-emission spectra corroborates with the UV absorptions (Fig. 1b) [15]. Further, in the PL study, maximum, the intense emission wavelength is observed at 435 nm when excited at 340 nm (Fig. S1d). Excitation studies at different wavelengths showed M-NCDs emission wavelength, and intensity strongly depends on the excitation wavelength. Such a pattern in PL spectra is observed owing to the non-uniform particle size and radiative recombination of carbonyl and nitrogen moieties [16,17]. The XRD analysis shows characteristic broad peak at 2θ = 22.8° which corresponds to the (002) plane of carbon dots (Fig. 1c). FT-IR spectrum, designated the occurrence of O–H/N–H groups (3300-3400 cm−1), acid carbonyl O=C–O (1712, 1610 cm−1), sp2 hybridized carbon (1445 cm−1), C–N (1320 cm−1), C–OH (1026 cm−1), and triazine unit (807 cm−1) (Fig. S1e). In Raman spectroscopy, two bands at 1352.26 cm−1 and 1562.29 cm−1 ascribed to the disordered sp3 and graphitic sp2 carbon (Fig. S1f). Chemical state and constitution M-NCDs are identified with the XPS study. C1s spectrum is deconvoluted to five peaks corresponding to C=C (289.3 eV), C=O (288.3 eV), C–O (286.5 eV), N–C=N (285.3 eV), and C–C (284.6 eV) (Fig. 1d). O1s peak is resolved into C=O (531.8eV) and C–O (532.5eV) (Fig. 1e). N1s peak comprises one peak C–N(399.9eV) it can be either graphitic one or the edge amino group (Fig. 1f) [18–20].

Using the relative fluorescence method with quinine sulfate as the reference sample (QY = 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4 at 380 nm), the quantum yield of M-NCD was calculated and found to be 14.9 %. Both storage time and continuous UV irradiation for a more extended period are used to investigate the stability of the doped carbon dots. With time, there was no discernible decrease in M-NCDs fluorescent intensity (Fig. S2a). Similarly, the material's photostability is also reasonably good. Even after 60 min of continuous UV exposure, a significant portion of the fluorescence intensity is still there (Fig. S2b).

Metal detection by M-NCDs was investigated towards ten metal samples. Only Hg(II) showed significant quenching of the fluorescence of M-NCDs in comparison to other metal ions (Fig. 2 a). When 50 μM Hg(II) solution was introduced to 30 g/mL of carbon nitride dot solution, the PL intensity dropped dramatically, indicating that binding of Hg(II) on to the surface of carbon dot, which sequentially supports the sensitivity of M-NCD towards mercuric ion. The relative fluorescence intensity of M-NCD did not significantly vary in an acid-base environment, confirming its stability. This pH analysis was necessary to determine the stability and pH-dependent quenching efficiency of Hg(II) with M-NCD. However, in the presence of Hg(II), at pH six, most fluorescence quenching was observed (Fig. S3a). Due to the maximum fluorescent quenching observed at pH 6, further studies were carried out. The fluctuation of M-NCDs fluorescence intensity with an increase in Hg(II) concentration was systematically studied from 30 nM to 100 nM. Fluorescence intensity moderately decreased as Hg(II) concentration increased. Additionally, no peak shifts were noticed, which are typically caused by scattering, photobleaching, etc., in some quantum dots (Fig. 2b). I0/I were plotted against Hg(II) concentration, and a linear curve was obtained between 30 nM and 100 nM (Fig. 2c), with an R2 value of 0.999. The limit of detection (LOD) of Hg(II) with M-NCDs is determined using the equation LOD =
3 σ / S
, where σ is the slope, and S is the standard deviation. From the linear plot given in Fig. 2c the slope and standard deviation are determined, based on these values the LOD for Hg(II) is calculated as 3.5 nM.

Furthermore, the fluorescent selectivity of M-NCD for Hg(II) was determined in the presence of other metal ions at a concentration double as that of Hg(II) (Fig.S4a), and the quenching due to other metal ions was negligible compared to Hg(II). The inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) can quench the fluorescence of a carbon dot [21]. They are in charge of the energy transfers that lead to fluorescence amplification or quenching. Because of the energy transfer between the excited state carbon dot and the ground state quencher in FRET, the lifetime of carbon dots will fluctuate. On the other hand, the carbon dot's lifetime in IFE stays essentially the same [22]. Lifetime analysis of M-NCD in the presence and absence of Hg(II) measured 7.91ns and 8.0ns (Fig.S5a), which is almost the same. Therefore, it is clear that the inner filter effect is responsible for the fluorescence quenching in M-NCD. Slope of Stern-Volmer plot for static quenching will be lower at the higher temperature compared to the slope at lower temperature, and vice-versa for dynamic quenching. Based on this theory, we have done fluorescence quenching studies at two different temperatures 298K and 310K and the slopes were determined from the plot (Fig.S6). As expected based on the lifetime data, the lifetime is unaffected by quencher concentration in static quenching. The higher slope at 298K compared to the slope at 310K along with similar lifetime values further confirms the existence of static quenching with inner filter effect.

The M-NCD modified GCE electrode was used for electrochemical detection experiments with metal salt solutions. In cyclic voltammetry studies with potential between −1.0 and 1.0 V and a scan rate of 50 mV/s, M-NCDs showed a particular sensitivity towards Hg(II) ions with an overall enhancement of current when compared to current responses of other metal ions (Fig. 2d). Hg(II) has a greater affinity for amino and oxygen-containing groups on the surface of M-NCDs. It can bind to multiple ‘imine’ (C=N–C) and hydroxyl groups on the M-NCD surface, which is drop casted on GCE. This binding of metal ions on the modified electrode magnifies anodic and cathodic responses. Also, the cumulative effect of an increase in surface area and high electrical conductivity with the introduction of M-NCD in GCE leads to the detection of Hg(II) [23,24]. When the current responses of the modified electrode were compared at different pH, a maximum anodic peak was obtained at pH 6 (Fig. S3b), so further analysis was done at this optimized pH. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was employed for the determination of detection limit of Hg(II) with the developed sensor. There was an increase in feedback current with an increase in Hg(II) concentration from 10 nM to 70 nM in DPV (Fig. 2e). From the linear plot so obtained by plotting the concentration of metal ion versus peak current, the detection limit of Hg(II) was calculated as 6.1 nM (Fig. 2f). A comparison of present work with similar reported materials in Hg(II) detection given inTable 1 (SI). The behavior of the modified electrode in response to potential interferences during the metal ion detection was evaluated using the Chronoamperometric technique from 0 to 180 s (Fig. S4b). Metal ions like Cr(VI), Pb(II), Fe(II), Ni(II), Fe(III), Cu(II), Mn(II), Cd(II), Co(II), Cr(III), Ag(I) and Zn(II) were used as interferents in selective Hg(II) detection. Due to the unique electron transfer interaction between the modified electrode surface and Hg(II) ions, cyclic voltammetry study revealed the preferential detection of Hg(II) (Fig. S5b). Two distinct peak currents, positive and negative distinguish the oxidation and reduction processes. While the positive peak current is brought on by the oxidation of Hg(0), the negative peak current is brought on by reducing Hg(II) ions on the electrode surface. This electron mobilization between M-NCD modified electrode and the metal salt solution explains the mechanism of electrochemical detection of Hg(II).

M-NCDs effectiveness for Hg(II) detection in real water was evaluated using real water samples. A few samples (lake and tap water) were collected and spiked with Hg(II) in known quantities. Additionally, fluorescence and electrochemical methods were used to analyze these spiked samples. The recovery percentage of Hg(II) in the various water samples ranged from 99.7 to 100.58 % (Table 2, SI). These findings imply that M-NCDs could work as appropriate real scenario probes for dual mode detection of Hg(II).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic degradation of malachite green with M-NCD
ce:section.ce:para:
The catalytic effectiveness of M-NCD was investigated by the breakdown of the potentially hazardous dye malachite green. The material's time-dependent MG degradation is depicted in Fig. 3 a. The catalyst can eradicate MG within 60 min without any co-catalyst and catalytic driving factors like light or heat. The kinetics of the degradation was also investigated (Fig. 3b). The pseudo-first order reaction kinetics is represented by the equation,
[3]
ln
C o − I n C = k t … … … …
where t is the irradiation time (min), k is the apparent reaction rate constant (min-1), C is the final concentration of MG (mg L-1) after the reaction, and C0 is the initial concentration of MG (mg L-1) before the reaction. It was discovered that catalytic degradation of MG followed the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics in good agreement with the literature [24]. To determine the role of M-NCD in MG degradation and to make sure light has no particular effect on the degradation process, a comparative study was performed with MG alone (control) and one M-NCD/MG system in the dark as well as one in solar light (Fig. 3c). No change in MG concentration was observed when it stood alone, specified the significant role of M-NCD in the dye breakdown. And the same degree of degradation displayed by the two systems indicated that the light has no particular implication on the reaction process.

The effect of catalyst loading and concentration of dye was further studied. When the M-NCD concentration varied from 200 to 1000 ppm, there was a pronounced rise in the reaction rate at higher catalyst concentrations. At lower catalyst loading, the percentage degradation was much lower than the optimized catalyst concentration but well above 70 %. The same observation was applied to the effect of dye concentration in the reaction (Fig. S7a). The critical variable that regulates the surface characteristics of the catalyst and the specific nature of the dissolved ion is the pH of any chemical reaction system. So the impact of pH on degradation was monitored from 5 to 9 (Fig. S7b). In the acidic range, the reaction occurred quite slowly, and from neutral to alkaline pH, the enhancement in the degradation was visually observable with fast discoloration. A systematic degradation can be observed at pH seven, unlike the alkaline pHs. So the neutral pH could be chosen as the optimum condition for MG degradation with M-NCD.

In addition to the dye discoloration observation and UV–vis. absorption spectra analysis, degradation of MG with time was further confirmed with HPLC analysis. The HPLC data was in agreement with the other observations. The intensity of MG solution collected halfway through the reaction showed a considerable reduction in intensity compared to the standard MG. Also, there was no peak corresponding to dye at 60 min, implying complete removal of MG (Fig. 4 a). Mechanism of degradation could be Fenton like Mechanism, here M-NCDs are generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) like hydroxyl radical (OH˙), and perhyroxyl radical (HOO˙). It is a known fact that carbon dots can generate reactive oxygen species, which imparts its anticancer and antibacterial properties. Here the ROS-radicals thus formed reacted with MG resulting in its complete breakdown (Fig. 4b) [25,26].

High-resolution mass spectrometry was used to look at the MG degradation pathway and identification of degradation products (Fig. 5 ). The MG samples were collected at various time intervals, and mass spectra were taken. Over 60 min, it has seen that malachite green with m/z = 364.91 degrades into harmless by-products such as alkyl chain molecules, CO2, and water (Fig. S8). Based on all the above observations, M-NCDs may act as a excellent catalyst in the whole transformation of MG into non-hazardous compounds. The main advantage of the catalyst is that it is a plain carbon dot without any metal or polymer decoration, bringing exhaustive degradation of MG. In addition to its most straightforward nature, it does not require external factors like light, heat, co-catalyst, etc., to break down such a complex dye structure in 60 min. The only drawback with the catalyst is that it’s highly dispersible in water. Therefore, the entire process is a homogenous catalytic reaction with 0 % catalyst recovery.

Even though M-NCDs efficiently degraded MG on their own, the effect of co-catalyst like H2O2 and NaBH4 on the rate of MG degradation was studied at their different concentrations. The Fenton process causes the dye to degrade in the presence of H2O2. As anticipated, the dye decolorization at the beginning of the process was sped up by the rise in H2O2 concentration (Fig. S9a). The impact of additional produced OH• radicals can be used to explain this. In this view, the concentration of NaBH4 was also altered to find the impact on the degradation of MG since the amount of NaBH4 affects the reduction of dye compounds. The degradation of the dye was shown to increase with increasing NaBH4 concentration. The rate of the MG degradation was so fast with NaBH4 that there was a complete removal of dye within 2–12 min, depending on its concentrations. A larger NaBH4 concentration augmented the local electron density on M-NCDs surface, which would speed up the process (Fig. S9b).

Significant stages in the degradation of MG with M-NCD are illustrated in Fig. 6 . Hydroxyl radical, perhyroxyl radical, and superoxide radicals are active species in the dye degradation. The catalytic degradation of MG molecules involves three critical steps:
(i)
Effective adsorption of MG molecules,
(ii)
Production of the oxidizing and reducing radicals, and
(iii)
The action of active radical species on MG leads to its gradual breakdown

Although HOO˙ is an efficient oxidant and encourages radical chain reactions, its oxidation potential is substantially lower than OH˙. Organic substrates oxidative destruction only happens through interaction with hydroxyl radicals, and the hydroperoxyl radicals are far less reactive. They do not significantly contribute to dye breakdown. In addition to OH˙ generation from molecular cleavage of water, it is also generated from superoxide radicals [25,27,28]. Visually it can be observed that the dark cyan color of MG slowly converts to green by the mid of the reaction, and later it transforms into a light yellow-colored solution.

The current work briefly discusses the possibility of fabricating nitrogen-doped carbon dots that are cost-effective, environmentally friendly, and have high quantum yield as a versatile nanomaterial with numerous applications, such as a dual sensor viz. fluorescent and electrochemical for the selective and sensitive detection of heavy metal Hg(II) ion. In addition to its excellent sensitivity towards Hg(II), M-NCDs could perform as a catalyst for the degradation of MG dye. M-NCDs performed exceptionally with a Hg(II) detection limit of 3.5 nM and 6.1 nM in the fluorescence and electrochemical metal ion detection studies. M-NCDs accomplished the same Hg(II) sensitivity in real water scenarios as in laboratory studies. A new phase of M-NCD was seen with the degradation of toxic MG dye. Optimization of different parameter studies displayed the potency of M-NCD to act as a catalyst for the comprehensive breakdown of complex dye structures. In its simple form, these nitrogen-doped carbon dots can contribute to environmental sustainability at two different levels.
Chinnu R Thara: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Beena Mathew: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Chinnu Thara is grateful to Council of Scientific & Industrial Research (CSIR) for Senior Research Fellowship (Fellowship No: 09/499(0105)/2018-EMR-I) and Department of Science & Technology (DST), Government of India for instrumentation facilities provided by DST-PURSE Phase II to Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala, India.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.125278.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots for photocatalytic degradation of aqueous pollutants: recent advancements
Adv. Opt. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Akbar, E. Moretti, and A. Vomiero, “Carbon Dots for Photocatalytic Degradation of Aqueous Pollutants: Recent Advancements,” Adv. Opt. Mater., vol. 9, no. 17, Sep. 2021, doi: 10.1002/ADOM.202100532.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A low-temperature solid-phase method to synthesize highly fluorescent carbon nitride dots with tunable emission
Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhou, Y. Yang, and C. Y. Zhang, “A low-temperature solid-phase method to synthesize highly fluorescent carbon nitride dots with tunable emission,” Chem. Commun., vol. 49, no. 77, pp. 8605-8607, Aug. 2013, doi: 10.1039/C3CC42266F.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Potential risk of mercury to human health in three species of fish from the southern Caspian Sea
Mar. Pollut. Bull.
